content.text: Labels and fieldsets are the two semantic mechanisms HTML provides for connecting explanatory text to form controls. A correctly labelled form is navigable by keyboard users, operable by screen reader users, and compliant with WCAG 2.1 criterion 1.3.1 (Info and Relationships). This topic covers every association technique, when to choose each one, and how Angular's reactive and template-driven forms interact with them.

attrs.alt: HTML Labels & Fieldsets anatomy — three label association methods, fieldset+legend structure, and best practices
attrs.title: HTML Labels & Fieldsets Anatomy

content.text: The label Element

The <label> element does two concrete things: it announces a human-readable name to assistive technologies when a control receives focus, and it extends the clickable area of the control to include the label text — useful for small checkboxes and radios.
Method 1: Explicit association (for / id)
The for attribute on the label must match the id attribute on the input. This is the most widely supported technique and works even when the label and input are not adjacent in the DOM.

Your display name

Pick a short identifier.
The id must be unique on the page. Duplicate ids break the association.
Multiple labels can point to the same input — all are announced by screen readers.
The for/id technique works across Shadow DOM boundaries when the input is in the light DOM.
Method 2: Wrapping label (implicit association)
When the label wraps the input, no id is required. This reduces the number of attributes to maintain and keeps related markup together.
Wrapping is slightly less supported in very old assistive technology, but all modern screen readers handle it correctly.
Do not nest interactive elements (buttons, links) inside a label — they confuse screen readers.
CSS for the label's layout does not affect the programmatic association — you can style the label text with a span freely.
Method 3: aria-label and aria-labelledby
When no visible label text exists — an icon-only search bar, a table cell input — use ARIA attributes to supply the accessible name directly.

Billing address

Quantity (1-100)

aria-labelledby takes precedence over a <label for> association. aria-label takes precedence over everything including aria-labelledby. Use native <label> first; reach for ARIA only when no visible text can serve as the label.
fieldset and legend

<fieldset> groups related controls, giving them a shared accessible context. <legend> is the first child of fieldset and provides the group's name — screen readers typically announce both the legend and the control's own label when focus enters a grouped control.
Radio button groups
This is the canonical use case. Without fieldset+legend, a screen reader announces each radio label in isolation and the user may not know they belong to a question.

<fieldset>
  <legend>Preferred contact method</legend>

  <label>
    <input type="radio" name="contact" value="email" checked />
    Email
  </label>
  <label>
    <input type="radio" name="contact" value="phone" />
    Phone
  </label>
  <label>
    <input type="radio" name="contact" value="sms" />
    SMS
  </label>
</fieldset>
A screen reader announces: "Preferred contact method, Email, radio button, 1 of 3". Without the fieldset the announcement is just "Email, radio button, 1 of 3" — the question is missing.
Checkbox groups

<fieldset>
  <legend>Notification preferences</legend>

  <label>
    <input type="checkbox" name="notif" value="email" />
    Email notifications
  </label>
  <label>
    <input type="checkbox" name="notif" value="push" />
    Push notifications
  </label>
  <label>
    <input type="checkbox" name="notif" value="sms" />
    SMS notifications
  </label>
</fieldset>
Grouping unrelated sections of a long form

<fieldset>
  <legend>Personal information</legend>
  <label for="fname">First name</label>
  <input id="fname" type="text" name="fname" autocomplete="given-name" />
  <label for="lname">Last name</label>
  <input id="lname" type="text" name="lname" autocomplete="family-name" />
</fieldset>

<fieldset>
  <legend>Billing address</legend>
  <label for="street">Street</label>
  <input id="street" type="text" name="street" autocomplete="street-address" />
  <label for="city">City</label>
  <input id="city" type="text" name="city" autocomplete="address-level2" />
</fieldset>
fieldset renders with a visual border by default. This can be reset with CSS (border: none; padding: 0; margin: 0) — the accessibility grouping is preserved regardless of styling.
Multiple fieldsets may appear in one form; they do not need to be contiguous.
legend must be the first child of fieldset — placing it elsewhere is invalid HTML and breaks the association.
fieldset + legend is appropriate for radio/checkbox groups; for simple text inputs, section headings with aria-labelledby on a containing div are sufficient.
Labels in Angular Forms

Angular reactive and template-driven forms do not generate label elements — you write them yourself. The association rules are identical to plain HTML. Angular's role is purely in form control binding.
Reactive form — full labeled example

import { Component } from '@angular/core'; import { FormGroup, FormControl, Validators, ReactiveFormsModule } from '@angular/forms'; import { CommonModule } from '@angular/common'; @Component({ selector: 'app-profile-form', standalone: true, imports: [CommonModule, ReactiveFormsModule], template: `
Personal details
Name is required.
Valid email required.
Notification preferences
Account type *
`, }) export class ProfileFormComponent { form = new FormGroup({ name: new FormControl('', Validators.required), email: new FormControl('', [Validators.required, Validators.email]), emailNotif: new FormControl(true), pushNotif: new FormControl(false), accountType: new FormControl('personal', Validators.required), }); showErr(name: string): boolean { const c = this.form.get(name); return !!(c?.invalid && c?.touched); } save(): void { this.form.markAllAsTouched(); if (this.form.valid) console.log(this.form.value); } }
Template-driven form — same structure
Personal details Name is required. Valid email required.
Placeholder vs Label: Why Placeholder Is Not a Label
Comparison
label element
placeholder attribute
Persists after typing
Yes — always visible
No — disappears on first character
Screen reader support
Consistently announced on focus
Announced on focus; not re-announced on error
Contrast requirements
Must meet WCAG 4.5:1 (text)
Often exempt (considered hint text); typically low-contrast
Can include formatting hints
Yes — add aria-describedby hint span
Yes — but disappears when typing starts
WCAG requirement
Required for accessible name (1.3.1, 1.4.5)
Not sufficient alone as accessible name
Use placeholder only for supplementary hints like an example value (e.g. placeholder="e.g. john@example.com").
Never use placeholder as the only label — if the user tabs past the field and tabs back, they cannot see what they typed.
If a field must look minimal (no visible label), use visually-hidden CSS and a real label: .sr-only { position:absolute; width:1px; height:1px; overflow:hidden; clip:rect(0 0 0 0); }
Required Field Conventions
Communicate which fields are required visually and programmatically.
* Required fields
required
attribute sets validity.valueMissing in the Constraint Validation API and tells screen readers the field is required.
aria-required="true"
is redundant when native required is present, but some teams add it for clarity or when using custom components that don't use native elements.
Mark optional fields instead (with "(optional)") if most fields are required — this reduces visual clutter.
Do not rely on color alone (e.g. red asterisk) without a text explanation — WCAG 1.4.1.
Styling Labels and Fieldsets
Common Pitfalls
Mismatched for/id.
A typo in the id or for attribute silently breaks the association. Test by clicking the label text — focus should move to the input.
Duplicate ids on the page.
React key, Angular trackBy, and server-side rendering can all produce duplicate ids if you generate ids from indices. Use unique values like UUIDs or form-field-${controlName}.
Legend not first in fieldset.
The legend element must be the first child of fieldset. Placing it elsewhere is invalid HTML and the border notch will not render correctly.
Nested <button> or <a> inside <label>.
Clicking a link inside a label activates the link, not the form control. Keep label content to text and inline elements only.
Missing fieldset for radio/checkbox groups.
Without a fieldset, each radio button is announced without context. Users navigating by form element cannot tell which question the button answers.
Placeholder-only labels in Angular.
Angular's formControlName does not generate a label — you must write it yourself. A common mistake is to rely on placeholder when converting a native HTML form to Angular.
Summary
Three association techniques: explicit (for/id), wrapping label, and aria-label/aria-labelledby. Prefer native <label> over ARIA.
Clicking a label moves focus to the associated control, improving usability for small targets like checkboxes and radios.
<fieldset> + <legend> groups related controls and announces the group name when a screen reader enters a control within it.
Use fieldset+legend for every radio and checkbox group — without it, the question context is lost for assistive technology users.
placeholder is a hint, not a label — it disappears on input, has relaxed contrast requirements, and is insufficient for WCAG compliance.
Mark required fields with the required attribute and a visible convention; explain the convention in a form-level note.
In Angular, write labels by hand — form directives (formControlName, ngModel) do not generate label elements.
